title: Vector Projection Quiz
description: Test your understanding of projecting vectors onto other vectors

questions.question: What does the dot product of two vectors tell us?

options.text: The area of the parallelogram they form
How much the vectors 'agree' in direction
The length of the longer vector

questions.explanation: The dot product measures directional agreement. When vectors point the same way, it's positive and large. When perpendicular, it's zero. When opposite, it's negative.
questions.hint: Think about what happens to the dot product when vectors are parallel vs perpendicular.

questions.question: The length of the projection of a onto b depends on the length of b.
questions.explanation: The projection only depends on b's direction, not its length. We normalize by |b|² in the formula specifically to remove this dependency. Try it in the interactive visualization!
questions.hint: What does dividing by |b|² do in the projection formula?

questions.question: If vectors a and b are perpendicular (at 90°), what is proj_b(a)?

options.text: A unit vector in b's direction

questions.explanation: When vectors are perpendicular, their dot product is zero (a · b = 0). Since the projection formula multiplies by this dot product, the entire projection becomes zero. There's no 'shadow' of a on b when they're at right angles.
questions.hint: What is a · b when the vectors are perpendicular?

questions.question: Complete the projection formula: proj_b(a) = ((a · b) / |b|²) × ____
questions.explanation: The formula has three parts: (1) compute a·b (directional agreement), (2) divide by |b|² (normalize), (3) multiply by b to get a vector in b's direction with the computed magnitude.
questions.hint: The result needs to be a vector pointing in which direction?

questions.question: Put these steps for computing vector projection in the correct order:

items.text: Multiply the result by vector b
Calculate the dot product a · b

questions.explanation: First we compute a·b to measure how much a aligns with b. Then we divide by |b|² to get a scalar (removing b's length from the equation). Finally, we multiply by b to convert our scalar back into a vector pointing in b's direction.
questions.hint: We need to compute a scalar first, then turn it into a vector.

questions.question: If the projection of a onto b points in the OPPOSITE direction of b, what does this tell us?

options.text: Vector a has a component opposing b's direction (angle > 90°)
Vector b is longer than vector a

questions.explanation: A negative projection means the dot product a·b was negative, which happens when the angle between vectors exceeds 90°. The projection points opposite to b because a is 'mostly' pointing away from b's direction.
questions.hint: When is the dot product negative?

questions.question: The dot product in vector projection works similarly to how Fourier transforms detect frequencies: both multiply corresponding components and sum the results.
questions.explanation: Great insight! In Fourier transforms, you multiply your signal by a sine wave at a specific frequency. If the signal contains that frequency, the products consistently add up (like aligned vectors). If not, positive and negative values cancel out (like perpendicular vectors). It's the same mathematical principle!
questions.hint: Think about what happens when you multiply two aligned sine waves vs two out-of-phase sine waves.

questions.question: The scalar (a·b)/|b|² in the projection formula represents:

options.text: How many 'b-lengths' fit into the projection of a onto b
The cosine of the angle between a and b
The area of the triangle formed by a and b

questions.explanation: This scalar tells us exactly how far along b the projection extends, measured in units of b's length. A scalar of 2 means the projection is twice as long as b. A scalar of 0.5 means it's half as long. A negative scalar means it points the opposite way.
questions.hint: Think of it like coordinates along a number line in b's direction.
